title: Paper contact card for children

content:
Practical guidance for “Paper contact card for children” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop condition: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Reduced fallback: If “Paper contact card for children” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.
materials: paper map and pencil — for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
official alert and route information — for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
contact and meeting-point card — for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
light, water, and essential medicine — for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
mobility and visibility aids — for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits. Record the result under “Navigation · Family · Movement” before continuing “Paper contact card for children”.

Mark hazards such as fire, smoke, floodwater, damaged bridges, unstable slopes, crowds, poor visibility, and restricted areas. Recheck this point before closing “Paper contact card for children” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Match pace, load, rest, assistance, and transport to children, older adults, disability, illness, animals, and the slowest safe member. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Paper contact card for children” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Leave a departure time, route, destination, group list, check-in schedule, and missed-contact rule with a trusted person. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Paper contact card for children”.

Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible. Include the outcome of “Paper contact card for children” in the handover for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
warnings: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Do not split the group without a written destination, communication rule, responsible adults, and reunification plan. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Paper contact card for children” unreliable: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible. alternatives: If “Paper contact card for children” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.
Use a dated paper note and spoken read-back for “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement” when the normal form, device, or communication channel is unavailable.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Paper contact card for children” when the route loses a safe exit, visibility, communications, daylight margin, water margin, or physical capacity. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop and reassess “Paper contact card for children” when a member is missing, distressed, injured, exhausted, confused, or unable to continue safely. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Paper contact card for children” while breathing, alertness, circulation, major bleeding, severe pain, seizure, poisoning, chest symptoms, pregnancy danger signs, or rapid deterioration is present. Apply this boundary to “Paper contact card for children” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

title: Pet evacuation information card
summary: Practical guidance for “Pet evacuation information card” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports animal welfare, soil and crop protection, clean feed and water, observation, and seasonal continuity, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.
content:
Practical guidance for “Pet evacuation information card” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports animal welfare, soil and crop protection, clean feed and water, observation, and seasonal continuity, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop condition: A frightened or injured animal may bite, kick, crush, scratch, or flee; protect people without abandoning welfare. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Reduced fallback: If “Pet evacuation information card” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller protected area, reduced herd or crop task, and qualified veterinary or agricultural advice.
materials: species-appropriate carrier, lead, or pen — for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
water and familiar feed — for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
identification and owner contact card — for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
clean bedding and waste bags — for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
veterinary and medicine record — for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Assess heat, smoke, floodwater, injury, escape, aggression, and disease risks before handling. Record the result under “Navigation · Family · Movement” before continuing “Pet evacuation information card”.

Use species-appropriate restraint with the least force and keep animals separate when stress or disease requires it. Recheck this point before closing “Pet evacuation information card” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Provide clean water, suitable feed, shade or warmth, ventilation, rest, and a dry waste-controlled area. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Pet evacuation information card” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Label carriers or pens and record identity, owner, medicines, feeding, elimination, behaviour, and destination. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Pet evacuation information card”.

Escalate early to a veterinarian, trained handler, authority, or rescue service for injury, poisoning, breathing trouble, birth complications, or uncontrolled behaviour. Include the outcome of “Pet evacuation information card” in the handover for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
warnings: A frightened or injured animal may bite, kick, crush, scratch, or flee; protect people without abandoning welfare. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Do not give human medicines, unfamiliar feed, or improvised doses unless a veterinarian has directed them. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Pet evacuation information card” unreliable: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: A frightened or injured animal may bite, kick, crush, scratch, or flee; protect people without abandoning welfare. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Escalate early to a veterinarian, trained handler, authority, or rescue service for injury, poisoning, breathing trouble, birth complications, or uncontrolled behaviour. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
alternatives: If “Pet evacuation information card” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller protected area, reduced herd or crop task, and qualified veterinary or agricultural advice.
Use a dated paper note and spoken read-back for “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement” when the normal form, device, or communication channel is unavailable.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Pet evacuation information card” when the animal develops breathing trouble, collapse, uncontrolled bleeding, repeated vomiting, severe swelling, seizures, or inability to stand. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop and reassess “Pet evacuation information card” when containment fails, several animals show similar illness, or feed, water, smoke, chemicals, or flood exposure is uncertain. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Pet evacuation information card” while an animal is aggressive, trapped in a dangerous structure, severely injured, showing neurological signs, or may expose people to bites, zoonotic disease, smoke, chemicals, or floodwater. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

This card is not authorization to use unsafe restraint, give human medicines, enter a hazardous area for an animal, or mix sick animals with the healthy group. Topic-specific stop check for “Pet evacuation information card”: also follow this warning: “A frightened or injured animal may bite, kick, crush, scratch, or flee; protect people without abandoning welfare in “Pet evacuation information card”.”. Apply this boundary to “Pet evacuation information card” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

long_term: Review “Pet evacuation information card” after real use in “Navigation · Family · Movement”; replace failed materials, practise the handover, and update thresholds when official guidance or local conditions change.
